description: CPU-only stdlib feasibility gate for Variant B (section-predictor + solver): join the 607 verified (rough, fine) pairs against the have-tree records and count how many verified decls expose a named subgoal frontier (>=1 / >=2 have-nodes, each carrying a ppType the predictor would emit).
motivation: A section-predictor can only be trained where the corpus exposes a named frontier to supervise on. Before any Modal/SFT spend on Variant B, test cheaply whether the current 607-pair corpus contains enough named have-frontiers.
formal_results: 607 verified pairs; 0 missing a have-tree record. have-node count distribution: 0 -> 565, 1 -> 15, 2 -> 12, 3 -> 7, 4 -> 4, 5 -> 1, 6 -> 2, 7 -> 1. With >=1 named have: 42 (6.9%); >=2: 27 (4.4%); >=3: 15 (2.5%). Among the 42 decls with any frontier: mean 2.38, median 2, max 7 have-nodes.
results_description: 93.1% (565/607) of verified proofs are flat (zero named have-nodes) -- nothing for a section-predictor to emit. Only 27 decls (4.4%) expose a non-trivial (>=2 subgoal) frontier; 42 (6.9%) expose any. This matches the ~4% decomposability ceiling found in e-0004 and the structural-supply problem behind q-0008.
conclusions: Variant B (section-predictor + solver) has essentially no within-corpus training signal on the current corpus: ~27 supervised frontiers is far too few to SFT a predictor. q-0009 is non-viable on data/corpus_v3 as-is; like q-0008 it is blocked on the same frontier-shrinking re-extraction (F_size expansion, which e-0004 showed raises decomposable counts) -- a real Modal pipeline change, not a cheap step.
